author: Sangharakshita
title: Contents of Precepts of the Gurus - 4th Seminar
content:
SANGHARAKSHITA IN SEMINAR

THE PRECEPTS OF THE GURUS - FOURTH SEMINAR
[Study based on the Chapter entitled:
'The Supreme Path, The Rosary of Precious Gems'
found in
"A BUDDHIST BIBLE", edited by Dwight Goddard,
1970 edition, Beacon Press, Boston, USA.
(ISBN 0-8070-5951-X)]
CONTENTS


'The Ten Resemblances wherein one may err'
(1) Desire may be mistaken for faith
'Desiring' to Go for Refuge
Initially seeing the Spiritual Community as a group
Desire for the Sangha
Desire for the Buddha
Seeing Buddhahood as a position to be appropriated
The Buddha and the cakravartiraja (wheel turning king)
Not being able to distinguish between positive qualities in their mundane sense and their transcendental sense
Desire for the Dharma
Mistaking desire for faith
You cannot have enlightenment for yourself
() Attachment many be mistaken for benevolence and compassion
'Strong' emotions
Metta and attachment
Benevolence and attachment
Metta should spark off metta
The difference between benevolence and compassion
Metta, karuna and mudita
Parents giving to children not out of genuine positive emotion
(3) Cessation of thought processes may be mistaken for the quiescence of infinite mind, which is the true goal.
Misunderstandings about what meditation is
Passing from the first to the second dhyana
From Samatha to Vipassana
Three thought processes ...
1. as a hindrance
2. as a support for the development of Insight
3. related to the means of expression of Insight
The second dhyana
Meditation being more dynamic and more truly calm
What is discursive thought?
Gautama the Buddha's discovery of the path of vipassana
Vipassana without a prior samatha experience - 'dry vipassana'
(4) Sense perceptions (or phenomena) may be mistaken for revelations (or glimpses) of Reality
Three types of vision (similar to the three types of thought process listed above)
(5) A mere glimpse of Reality may be mistaken for complete realization
Not settling down in any experience - even Enlightenment
Questioning every experience
'You are either going forward or going backward'
Mara
Next Session (6) Those who outwardly profess, but do not practise, religion may be mistaken for true devotees.
Distinctive dress for particular vocations/professions
A religion becoming part of the 'establishment'
Living in a community is a sure way of testing if people are only outwardly professing to be 'religious'
Integrating our desire to meditate/practise with meditating/practising and vice-versa!
The clergyman as a figure of fun
Bethnal Green is officially the least Christian part of Britain!
Not seeing the Christian Church as a spiritual body generally
(7) Slaves of passion may be mistaken for masters of yoga who have liberated themselves from all conventional laws.
Tantric yogis liberated from conventional laws i.e. by 'killing' someone
Not sional and natural morality
Going by one's own experience
(8) Actions performed in the interest of self may be mistakenly regarded as being altruistic.
Not questioning peoples' motives too readily
Thinking about how to vote at political elections, national and local
Buddhists becoming elected on to local borough councils
(9) Deceptive methods may be mistakenly regarded as being prudent.
The four moral virtues - justice, temperance, prudence and fortitude
Being honest in business dealings
'Deceiving' an institution
Taxation and some objections to it
The belief of some groups, including Christians, that violence is morally justified
Religious organizations functioning on neurotic energy
(10) Charlatans may be mistaken for Sages.
A Pali or Sanskrit word for 'individual'?
The Pudgalavadins being heretical?
The charlatan as the group leader
The next session ...
'The Ten Things Wherein One Erreth Not'
(1) In being free from attachment to all objects, and being ordained a bhikshu into the Holy Order, forsaking home and entering upon the homeless state, one doth not err.
Going Forth from the group
Going Forth from the parental group
Can we Go Forth from the National group?
Learning other languages
Catholics and the effect of the Virgin Mary, especially on women
'You cannot leave the (Catholic) Church
Trying to get away from the Indian caste system
The class system in Britain and class mobility
Balancing mental work and physical work
Ideological gender stereotypes
Going forth in all sorts of ways all of the time
The danger of settling down and the danger of restlessness (in communities)
(2) In revering one's spiritual preceptor one doth not err.
Spiritual communities themselves have their ups and downs
Metta in contact with something spiritually superior becomes faith or reverence
Metta as the foundation of all tine metta
Reacting to the group is not necessarily Going Forth from it
(3) In thoroughly studying the Doctrine, hearing discourses thereon, and reflecting and meditating upon it, one doth not err.
Listening, reflecting and meditating savanamaya, cintamaya and bhanavanamaya panna (Pali)
(4) In nourishing lofty aspirations and a lowly demeanour one doth not err.
False humility
(5) In entertaining liberal views (as to religion) and yet being firm in observing (formal religious) vows one doth not err.
Being liberal and not really caring
Buddhists worshipping Hindu gods?
A split between reason and emotion in the Theravada
(6) In having greatness of intellect and smallness of pride, one doth not err.
Knowing more than you are
A bloated intellect, not a developed one
Pride used in its positive sense
English literature. Discovering and rediscovering the Classics
Next Session
(7) In being wealthy in religious doctrines and diligent in meditating upon them one doth not err.
Not regarding lectures and study as entertainment
Knowing the doctrines simply because one is interested in them, not to attain a
Buddhist qualification
(8) In having profound religious learning, combined with knowledge of things spiritual and absence of pride, on doth not err.
Profound religious learning
Each of the major Mahayana Sutras is a world in itself
How much do you need to study?
More and more of less and less
The significance and function of The Precepts of the Gurus
The oral tradition in Buddhist teaching
Reading too much but not studying enough
Building up a personal library of, say, 10 or 12 books
Knowing a few texts extremely well - even by heart
(9) By passing one's whole life in solitude and meditation, one doth not err.
Solitude leads to naturally meditating
What is a completely solitary retreat?
Absence of all external distraction
Solitude being seen as a punishment
Talking to the cat in silent periods on retreat
The imagination of children
(10) In being unselfishly devoted to doing good to others, by means of wise
methods, one doth not err.
Taking on the sufferings of the world
Needing to be stable and psychologically positive if dealing with people in disturbed states
Trying to help people who do not really want to be helped
The need for more skilled/specialized people in the FWBO
The difficulty of trying to combine karate and meditation
A need for consensus on what needs to be done (in communities)
General impressions of the study
Did Bhante have any problems when he threw away his passport when in
India? Forming positive habits
